title: Bump handlebars from 4.0.12 to 4.1.2
bodyText:
Bumps handlebars from 4.0.12 to 4.1.2.

Changelog
Sourced from handlebars's changelog.

v4.1.2 - April 13th, 2019
Chore/Test:

#1515 - Port over linting and test for typings (@​zimmi88)
chore: add missing typescript dependency, add package-lock.json - 594f1e3
test: remove safari from saucelabs - 871accc

Bugfixes:

fix: prevent RCE through the "lookup"-helper - cd38583

Compatibility notes:
Access to the constructor of a class thought {{lookup obj "constructor" }} is now prohibited. This closes
a leak that only half closed in versions 4.0.13 and 4.1.0, but it is a slight incompatibility.
This kind of access is not the intended use of Handlebars and leads to the vulnerability described
in #1495. We will not increase the major version, because such use is not intended or documented,
and because of the potential impact of the issue (we fear that most people won't use a new major version
and the issue may not be resolved on many systems).
Commits

Bugfixes:

fix: add "runtime.d.ts" to allow "require('handlebars/runtime')" in TypeScript - 5cedd62

Refactorings:

use "substring"-function instead of "substr" - 445ae12

Compatibility notes:

This is a bugfix release. There are no breaking change and no new features.

title: added fortmatic, fixed ledger timeout
bodyText:
This PR adds the Fortmatic provider.
It also adds low-level geth RPC overrides into the traceMethodCalls function inside the getSigner module. Ethers.js tries to be too clever and makes abstraction hard, so this is my way of side-stepping that cleverness with standard functions. Every single provider needs to be tested before this is shipped to production. I have tested them myself, but would prefer some redundancy.
Blocktracker is also moved into its own module. This is in preparation for some refactoring in the way events are handled.
Finally, the ledger node_module is also updated. The 3 second timeout issue everyone was experiencing seems to be fixed in the latest version.

title: Added system wide balance tracking of ETH and ERC20s
bodyText:
This update will trigger balance updates when any block contains direct ETH transfers to or from addresses of interest to the system (logged in taker address + connected maker addresses) or any transaction that triggers an ERC20 transfer event (normal transfers, orders filled on airswap or a different dex, compound, cdp, etc).
Next steps would be:

abstraction around the tracked addresses, being able to exclude automated makers or include an additional arbitrary address of a taker as would be used in an OTC application
more fine grain control in deltabalances to exclude checking the balance of tokens we can safely not care about
moving the deltaBalances contract calls into the gethRead abstractions to guarantee synchronicity between potentially out of sync geth data sources, and to fully leverage websockets where appropriate for faster load times.

title: Generalized balance tracking
bodyText:
Added IS_INSTANT env variable, if it evaluates to true it toggles on all redux functionality associated with instant liquidity. If it is falsey (like it will be in the OTC app), all the extra instant functionality is turned off by default. This functionality includes:

CONNECT_WALLET action also initiates a connection to the router
turning on polling of API values of users currently connected to the router, current intents, real-time balance & approval tracking of all makers



Added the concept of trackedAddresses to deltabalances. A trackedAddresses is stored as an array of pairs [{ tokenAddress, address },...] where address is a wallet address. This list can be used by any dapp to add real time balance tracking of a wallet address and associated assets to the event blockCruncher.


Also added a call abstraction to gethRead to allow for websocket updates of deltabalance info (or any contract data read, just use the Proxy'd ethers JSON RPC provider as defined here: https://github.com/airswap/AirSwap.js/blob/sam-working/src/deltaBalances/index.js#L32. This now means that 100% of ethereum data fed into the app comes from gethRead, which can be configured to use any provider you want (nodesmith websocket, alchemy websocket, perhaps ipc provider)

title: ENS support part 2
bodyText: Second attempt at ENS support, this time using a forked version of the ENS domains UI library so we can use our app's ethers provider for better stability

title: Migrated event tracking to ABI driven generative redux tooling
bodyText:
every contract smart module (swap, swapLegacy) can now have redux interface functions generated for them in eventTrackingSelectors.js and eventTrackingActions.js. The tooling for this lives in src/utils/generateReduxFromABI.js. This gives a concise API that allows you to specify exactly what you want tracked (plus the ability to filter to indexed parameters for speed/optimization), for what block range (i.e. backfilling 7000 blocks for 24 hour volume, etc), and also generates the selectors for these event queries.
(also sorry for accidentally pushing straight to develop >_<)

bodyText:

It also transitions the swap function on the swap contract to the new transaction tracking paradigm. This new paradigm is highly scalable and will allow us to rapidly integrate many new functions / contracts. swap is the initial POC, and will be followed by delegate authorization, and then wrapped swap.

title: abiGen refactoring
bodyText:
moved generateContractFunctions into an /abiGen submodule. Moved abiGen utils into a /abiGen/utils.js submodule. Trying to get away from the large flat file that is currently the abiGeneration code into something more maintainable / documentable.
changed generateContractFunctions generated files to use pure commonjs exports. Mixed module declarations were causing issues when trying to include these functions in nodejs code (makers)
introduced a alchemyWeb3 event subscription module websocketEventTracker. The fallback http event tracking module eventTracker will still be available. I don't want the codebase to become dependent on websocket subscriptions. Http will be available as a fallback, even if it's slow.
updated to the latest wrapper contract address.

title: Indexer Basic Tests
bodyText:
HEY! Are you making a PR against master?
DO NOT MERGE TO MASTER WITHOUT BUMPING THE VERSION FIRST IN package.json
.env should be:
PRIVATE_KEY="0x<private_key>"
ALCHEMY_API_KEY="<alchemy_key>"
MOCHA_IS_TESTING="true"

Start up ganache-cli:
ganache-cli --account="$(node get_private_key.js),100000000000000000000" -f $(node get_alchemy.js)

Run Tests:
mocha indexer.test.js --exit --timeout 10000

Note: we fork mainnet due to ConsenSys-archive/ganache#706

title: added infura as default node provider
bodyText:
The app now has two main providers:

web3Provider, which is a websocket provider that handles subscriptions, and
ethersProvider, which is an http provider that handles contract function cals on contract objects generated by ethers

I tried to use the websocket provider for both but there seems to be a bug in the ethers implementation for websockets.
In order to activate infura as the default provider, simply remove the ALCHEMY keys from the .env files of the associated projects.

title: Token caching
bodyText: Adds ['tokens', 'data'] to localStorage blacklist and always uses fresh AirSwap token metadata on load.

title: Fixes connection issue in Firefox
bodyText:
Underlying connection in ethers JsonRpcProvider had been consistently failing. It appeared that the HTTP version was being incorrectly set. Instead of POST ... HTTP/2 the request would read POST ... undefined. Overriding the send function and using axios fixes the issue. If there's something lighter weight happy to check it out. Tested in Firefox and Chrome.
Also added a null check in setTokens in src/tokens/index.js.

title: Expect ethereum node URLs to be passed or use provider defaults
bodyText: Rather than figuring out a provider URL based on a variety of API keys (hard coded or env variables), this change just takes ETH_NODE_HTTP and ETH_NODE_WEBSOCKET from the host application environment. If those are not set, default providers are used.
